caveats: a: The occupational mix is for NAICS 333500, not six-digit 333517.
a: The estimate excludes already-automated CNC control and robotic functions from the unautomated opportunity.
rho: Hurco is a public multinational rather than an LMM sample.
rho: Digital features embedded in sold machines do not automatically substitute labor inside the manufacturer.
e: The provided count is margin-bridged using a sector margin and may misclassify firms into the EBITDA band.
e: Some custom builders may have repeat customers but highly project-dependent revenue.
s5: No six-digit qualifying-transfer denominator was found.
s5: Succession readiness does not measure completed control transactions.
q: Hurco confirms aftermarket activities but does not measure benefit retention.
q: Capture can be higher in proprietary installed-base parts and lower in competitively bid new machines.
d5: USMTO values are nominal and one rebound year does not establish a five-year trend.
d5: The turning-machine forecast covers one segment and is commercially produced.
d5: New equipment and aftermarket demand behave differently.
o: Operator requirement can migrate to foreign or larger builders without disappearing.
o: Software-defined functionality may reduce some hardware options while increasing control and support work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Capital-equipment sales through direct and distributor channels, often supplemented by recurring replacement parts, control or software upgrades, automation integration, training, applications support, and service.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal machine-building units, shells, hand tools, welding equipment, rolling-mill machinery, cutting tools and accessories, and stand-alone distributors or service firms classified outside NAICS 333517.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying metal-cutting and metal-forming machine tools, including lathes, mills, grinders, drilling, punching, shearing, bending, pressing, forging, and die-casting machines, plus associated parts, upgrades, training, and support to external industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 333500 metalworking-machinery occupation mix, where production occupations were 59.81% of employment in May 2023; Census notes that design is part of machinery production; and NIST documents AI uses in inspection, maintenance, forecasting, and process decisions. I assign high exposure to configuration, quoting, documentation, planning, purchasing, service triage, and software work; partial exposure to design, controls, applications engineering, programming, quality, and supervision; and low exposure to machining, welding, assembly, wiring, test execution, material handling, installation, and repair execution. The broader occupational mix may understate engineering in complex builders and overstate it in standardized machines.
caveats: The occupational mix is for NAICS 333500, not six-digit 333517.
The estimate excludes already-automated CNC control and robotic functions from the unautomated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory across 12-15 independent machine-tool builders, with time studies for configuration, engineering, programming, assembly, test, installation, parts, and support, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Machine tools contain substantial digital design, controls, configuration, and support work, but complex physical fabrication, assembly, commissioning, and field service remain labor-intensive.

d5.bridge: AMT reports $5.74 billion of US manufacturing-technology orders in 2025, 22.5% above 2024 after three years of decline, while a commercial US turning-machine forecast projects growth from $1.9 billion in 2026 to $2.4 billion in 2031 at 4.8% annually. I use these as cyclical and segment proxies, then temper them to constant-price five-year demand for all current metal-cutting and forming machines, parts, upgrades, and support. The rebound and nominal segment forecast likely overstate through-cycle real growth, while installed-base aftermarket is steadier.
caveats: USMTO values are nominal and one rebound year does not establish a five-year trend.
The turning-machine forecast covers one segment and is commercially produced.
New equipment and aftermarket demand behave differently.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted USMTO unit and value orders by machine type, installed-base age, utilization, replacement cycles, and builder backlog and cancellation data would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Replacement of aging equipment, automation, aerospace and defense, and domestic investment support demand, but machine tools are highly cyclical, long-lived capital goods exposed to imports, interest rates, tariffs, and customer utilization.

caveats: The provided count is margin-bridged using a sector margin and may misclassify firms into the EBITDA band.
Some custom builders may have repeat customers but highly project-dependent revenue.
e.change_evidence: Screen all 272 estimated in-band firms using commercial databases and direct calls for captive status, installed base, repeat parts or support revenue, ownership dependence, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, and product liability history.
e.rationale: Most in-band builders repeatedly serve external industrial customers and possess transferable designs, controls expertise, supplier networks, installed bases, and aftermarket relationships. Ineligibility arises from captive units, one-off owner-engineered shops, distress, weak support infrastructure, customer concentration, and firms placed in-band incorrectly by the margin bridge.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $2,940.8M / sectoral output (T30) $9,729.7M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $330,956 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333517); class <5: 476 firms, avg revenue/firm $549,975 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,905 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 221 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,570,339 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $308,101 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 152 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,043,776 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $597,189 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 211 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,155,076 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,796,226 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 61 firms, avg revenue/firm $33,422,836 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,557,560 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $107,877,725 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $21,165,610 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 272

caveats: Operator requirement can migrate to foreign or larger builders without disappearing.
Software-defined functionality may reduce some hardware options while increasing control and support work.
o.change_evidence: Customer capex and engineering interviews plus process-route, remanufacturing, self-integration, and additive-substitution data would quantify elimination and self-service of the current basket.
o.rationale: Industrial customers still require accountable builders to design, fabricate, assemble, integrate, test, install, and support physical machines. Software can simplify programming and service and can shift value toward controls, but cannot supply the machine structure, motion system, safety enclosure, or commissioning; some quantity can disappear through additive manufacturing, customer self-integration, remanufacturing, and longer equipment life.

caveats: Hurco confirms aftermarket activities but does not measure benefit retention.
Capture can be higher in proprietary installed-base parts and lower in competitively bid new machines.
q.change_evidence: Machine, parts, software, distributor, and service contracts plus five-year deal-level histories for list-to-net price, discounts, warranty, productivity guarantees, commissions, upgrades, and gross margin would reveal pass-through.
q.rationale: Installed-base compatibility, proprietary controls, applications knowledge, spare parts, switching cost, commissioning risk, and service response can preserve savings. New-machine bids, distributor economics, global competition, customer capital committees, warranty commitments, and periodic sourcing still share benefits over five years.

rho.bridge: NIST reports 46% manufacturing AI-tool use and more than 80% expecting increased use in two years, while listing data, cost, skills, cyber, and legacy integration barriers. Hurco documents computerized CNC products, proprietary controls and software, automation integration, upgrades, service, training, and applications support. I translate this broad adoption and digital-product context into the five-year implementation share of exposed tasks. Digitally mature builders may implement faster than general manufacturing, while small custom builders may lack standardized data.
caveats: Hurco is a public multinational rather than an LMM sample.
Digital features embedded in sold machines do not automatically substitute labor inside the manufacturer.
rho.change_evidence: Builder-level deployment records showing baseline labor, engineering change rates, service resolution, commissioning hours, safety validation, customer acceptance, and sustained savings would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Engineering assistants, configuration, scheduling, service knowledge, remote diagnostics, and inspection can deploy, but safety validation, machine liability, controls integration, long product lives, customer cybersecurity, and field variability prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Deloitte's survey of 500 US family-enterprise members found only 24% of current-generation respondents strongly agreed their succession plan could withstand departure of an important family employee. I use this as broad succession-pressure evidence, then reduce potential exits for internal transitions, non-control transactions, closures, and failed sales. The survey is not machine-tool-specific or a timing measure and may include larger, better-advised firms.
caveats: No six-digit qualifying-transfer denominator was found.
Succession readiness does not measure completed control transactions.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and exit-intention outreach to identified builders plus five years of closed control deals from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, AMT members, and industrial-equipment bankers would narrow the range.
s5.rationale: A sizeable specialist-builder population should create owner transitions, but volatile backlogs, product liability, customer support obligations, and owner-held engineering knowledge can prevent or delay a qualifying control transfer.

principal_driver: Engineering-rich workflows and installed-base aftermarket relationships create implementable and retainable opportunities.
principal_weakness: Highly cyclical new-equipment demand and safety-critical physical delivery limit consistency.

sources.supports: Defines metal-cutting and metal-forming machine-tool manufacturing and lists lathes, mills, grinders, drilling, bending, forming, punching, pressing, forging, and die-casting machines.
sources.title: Census NAICS definition: 333517 Machine Tool Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 97,070 production workers representing 59.81% of broader NAICS 333500 employment and provides occupation-specific employment and wage context.
sources.title: BLS May 2023 occupation estimates for Metalworking Machinery Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 46% manufacturing AI-tool use and more than 80% expecting increased use in two years, with manufacturing use cases and implementation barriers.
sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: Describes CNC machine tools, proprietary controls and software, automation integration, components, upgrades, accessories, replacement parts, customer service, training, and applications support.
sources.title: Hurco Companies 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Reports $5.74 billion of 2025 machinery orders, 22.5% above 2024, following three years of decline after the 2021 rebound.
sources.title: AMT: Manufacturing Technology Orders Set Record in December 2025

sources.supports: Describes a January 2024 survey of 500 US family-enterprise respondents and reports that 24% of current-generation respondents strongly agreed their succession plan would withstand departure of an important family employee.
sources.title: Deloitte Private survey: generational disparities in family-business succession planning

sources.supports: Forecasts the US turning-machine segment from $1.9 billion in 2026 to $2.4 billion in 2031 at 4.8% CAGR; used only as a commercial segment proxy.
sources.title: United States Turning Machine and Equipment Market forecast

title: Machine Tool Manufacturing
